member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_SeekIO(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_IOStream*,System.Int64,Sdl3Sharp.IO.StreamWhence)
member.summary: Seek within an SDL_IOStream data stream
member.param: A pointer to an SDL_IOStream structure
An offset in bytes, relative to whence location; can be negative
Any of SDL_IO_SEEK_SET, SDL_IO_SEEK_CUR, SDL_IO_SEEK_END
member.returns: Returns the final offset in the data stream after the seek or -1 on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information.

remarks.para: This function seeks to byte , relative to .

may be any of the following values:
SDL_IO_SEEK_SET Seek from the beginning of data
SDL_IO_SEEK_CUR Seek relative to current read point
SDL_IO_SEEK_END Seek relative to the end of data
If this stream can not seek, it will return -1.
This function is not thread safe.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_TellIO(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_IOStream*)
member.summary: Determine the current read/write offset in an SDL_IOStream data stream
member.param: An SDL_IOStream data stream object from which to get the current offset
member.returns: Returns the current offset in the stream, or -1 if the information can not be determined

remarks.para: SDL_TellIO is actually a wrapper function that calls the SDL_IOStream's seek method, with an offset of 0 bytes from SDL_IO_SEEK_CUR, to simplify application development.
This function is not thread safe.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_WriteIO(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream.SDL_IOStream*,System.Void*,System.UIntPtr)
member.summary: Write to an SDL_IOStream data stream
member.param: A pointer to an SDL_IOStream structure
A pointer to a buffer containing data to write
The number of bytes to write
member.returns: Returns the number of bytes written, which will be less than on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

remarks.para: This function writes exactly bytes from the area pointed at by to the stream. If this fails for any reason, it'll return less than to demonstrate how far the write progressed. On success, it returns .
On error, this function still attempts to write as much as possible, so it might return a positive value less than the requested write size.
The caller can use SDL_GetIOStatus() to determine if the problem is recoverable, such as a non-blocking write that can simply be retried later, or a fatal error.
This function is not thread safe.

member.returns: Returns true on successful write or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

remarks.para: SDL byteswaps the data only if necessary, so the application always specifies native format, and the data written will be in big-endian format.

member.summary: Provides property names for properties

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.StreamExtensions.ToClrStream(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,System.Boolean,System.Nullable{System.Boolean},System.Nullable{System.Boolean},System.Nullable{System.Boolean})
member.summary: Creates a .NET (CLR) wrapping the provided SDL
member.param: The SDL to wrap
A value indicating whether to leave the underlying SDL stream open when the wrapper is disposed
An overwrite flag indicating whether the stream can be read from
An overwrite flag indicating whether the stream can be seeked
An overwrite flag indicating whether the stream can be written to
member.returns: A wrapper around the SDL

remarks.para: The constructor does its best to detect the capabilities of the provided SDL . But in some cases it might not be possible to determine whether the stream can be read from, seeked or written to, or the detection might be wrong. In those cases, you can use the optional , and parameters to explicitly specify the capabilities of the stream. Note that, for example specifying for for an inherently read-only stream will not magically make it writable, but it will make this wrapper report that it is writable.
Please remember to dispose the returned instance when you are done using it to free up any resources.

member.exception: is

member.summary: Creates an SDL wrapping the provided .NET (CLR)

A value indicating whether to leave the underlying CLR stream open when the wrapper is disposed
member.returns: A wrapper around the CLR

remarks.para: Please remember to dispose the returned instance when you are done using it to free up any resources.

member.exception: is

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.StreamExtensions.TryCopyTo(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,System.UIntPtr@,System.UIntPtr@,System.Nullable{System.TimeSpan},System.UIntPtr)
member.param: The time to wait for the stream to become ready or fail if exceeded. Fractional nanoseconds will get rounded to next greater whole nanosecond value. Must not be negative and must represent a rounded nanoseconds value that is less or equal to . If , waits indefinitely. If , fails immediately.
member.exception: is negative or the rounded nanosecond value represents is greater than
The internal timer SDL uses may have been reset or overflowed during the execution. Only happens when is neither nor .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.StreamExtensions.TryCopyTo(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,System.UIntPtr@,System.UIntPtr@,System.Nullable{System.UInt32},System.UIntPtr)
member.param: The number of milliseconds to wait for the stream to become ready or fail if exceeded. If , waits indefinitely. If 0, fails immediately.
member.exception: The internal timer SDL uses may have been reset or overflowed during the execution. Only happens when is neither nor 0.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.StreamExtensions.TryCopyTo(Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,Sdl3Sharp.IO.Stream,System.UIntPtr@,System.UIntPtr@,System.Nullable{System.UInt64},System.UIntPtr)
member.summary: Tries to copy all data from the source stream into a specified destination stream
member.param: The source stream to copy data into
The destination stream to copy data into
The number of bytes read from the stream
The number of bytes written to the stream
The number of nanoseconds to wait for the stream to become ready or fail if exceeded. If , waits indefinitely. If 0, fails immediately. Defaults to 0.
The size of the buffer to use for copying. Defaults to 4096.
member.returns: , if the data from the stream was successfully copied to the stream; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: This method tries to copy as many bytes as possible before returning. will be equal to the actual number of bytes read from the stream, while will be equal to the actual number of bytes written to the stream, even in the case when not all data could be copied. You should check the stream's property as well as the 's property to determine whether a shortened copy operation is recoverable or not.

You may want to specify a timeout value for the time to wait for the stream to become ready in asynchronous I/O scenarios. You can specify 0 to fail immediately, if the stream is not ready yet. You can also specify to wait indefinitely for the stream to become ready. If the stream's property is any other value than or , this methods still fails and returns . Please notice that the specified timeout value is only for waiting on the stream; if the stream cannot read in time, this method still fails and returns (this behavior is identical to any other of the TryRead* methods of a , except this method still returns when the stream's property is and all data was successfully written).
This method is not threadsafe.

member.exception: is
is not greater than 0
The internal timer SDL uses may have been reset or overflowed during the execution. Only happens when is neither nor 0.

remarks.para: While a can generally be kept open throughout runtime, a should only be opened when the application is ready to read or write files. This allows the backend to properly batch file operations and flush them when the container has been closed; ensuring safe and optimal save I/O.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.IO.UserStorage.SDL_OpenUserStorage(System.Byte*,System.Byte*,System.UInt32)
member.summary: Opens up a container for a user's unique read/write filesystem
member.param: The name of your organization
The name of your application
A property list that may contain backend-specific information
member.returns: Returns a user storage container on success or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

remarks.para: While title storage can generally be kept open throughout runtime, user storage should only be opened when the client is ready to read/write files. This allows the backend to properly batch file operations and flush them when the container has been closed; ensuring safe and optimal save I/O.

member.name: T:Sdl3Sharp.Locale
member.summary: Represents locale identification data
member.param: The language identifier for the
The country identifier for the , or if there's none

remarks.para: Locale identification data is split into a spoken language, like English, and an optional country, like Canada.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.#ctor(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Represents locale identification data
member.param: The language identifier for the
The country identifier for the , or if there's none

remarks.para: Locale identification data is split into a spoken language, like English, and an optional country, like Canada.

member.name: P:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.Language
member.summary: Gets the language identifier for the
member.value: The language identifier for the

remarks.para: The value for this property will be in ISO-639 format, e.g. English would be "en".

member.name: P:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.Country
member.summary: Gets the country identifier for the
member.value: The country identifier for the , if there's any; otherwise,

remarks.para: The value for this property will be in ISO-3166 format, e.g. Canada would be "CA", if there's a country identifier to the ; otherwise, the value for this property will be .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.TryGetPreferredLocales(Sdl3Sharp.Locale[]@)
member.summary: Tries to get the user's preferred locales
member.param: The user's preferred locales when this method returns ; otherwise,
member.returns: , if the user's preferred locales could be successfully retrieved; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: The returned list of locales are in the order of the user's preference. For example, a German citizen that is fluent in US English and knows enough Japanese to navigate around Tokyo might have a list like: de, en-US, jp. Someone from England might prefer British English (where "color" is spelled "colour", etc), but will settle for anything like it: en-GB, en.
This method returns together with set to on failure, including when the platform does not supply preferred locale information at all.

This might be a "slow" call that has to query the operating system. It's best to ask for this once and save the results. However, this list can change, usually because the user has changed a system preference outside of your program; SDL will send an event (see also ) in this case, if possible, and you can call this method again to get an updated copy of preferred locales.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.Deconstruct(System.String@,System.String@)
member.summary: Deconstructs the into its language identifier part and its country identifier part
member.param: The language identifier for the
The country identifier for the , if there's any; otherwise,

remarks.para: The resulting value for the parameter will be in ISO-639 format, e.g. English would be "en".
The resulting value for the parameter will be in ISO-3166 format, e.g. Canada would be "CA", if there's a country identifier to the ; otherwise, the value will be .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Locale.SDL_GetPreferredLocales(System.Int32*)
member.summary: Report the user's preferred locale
member.param: A pointer filled in with the number of locales returned, may be NULL
member.returns: Returns a NULL terminated array of locale pointers, or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information. This is a single allocation that should be freed with SDL_free() when it is no longer needed.

remarks.para: Returned language strings are in the format xx, where 'xx' is an ISO-639 language specifier (such as "en" for English, "de" for German, etc). Country strings are in the format YY, where "YY" is an ISO-3166 country code (such as "US" for the United States, "CA" for Canada, etc). Country might be NULL if there's no specific guidance on them (so you might get { "en", "US" } for American English, but { "en", NULL } means "English language, generically"). Language strings are never NULL, except to terminate the array.
Please note that not all of these strings are 2 characters; some are three or more.

The returned list of locales are in the order of the user's preference. For example, a German citizen that is fluent in US English and knows enough Japanese to navigate around Tokyo might have a list like: { "de", "en_US", "jp", NULL }. Someone from England might prefer British English (where "color" is spelled "colour", etc), but will settle for anything like it: { "en_GB", "en", NULL }.
This function returns NULL on error, including when the platform does not supply this information at all.

This might be a "slow" call that has to query the operating system. It's best to ask for this once and save the results. However, this list can change, usually because the user has changed a system preference outside of your program; SDL will send an SDL_EVENT_LOCALE_CHANGED event in this case, if possible, and you can call this function again to get an updated copy of preferred locales.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Log.Critical(Sdl3Sharp.LogCategory,System.String)
member.summary: Logs a with
member.param: The category of the message
The message to be logged

remarks.para: The parameter is treated as a C-style printf format string. This means that any % characters are interpreted as format specifiers. To include a literal % character in the output, you must escape it by writing %%.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Log.Critical(Sdl3Sharp.LogCategory,System.String,System.ReadOnlySpan{System.Object})
member.summary: Logs a string with
member.param: The category of the message
The C-style printf format string
The arguments corresponding to the format specifiers in

remarks.para: The parameter is interpreted as a C-style printf format string, and the parameter supplies the values for its format specifiers. Supported argument types include all integral types up to 64-bit (including and ), floating point types ( and ), pointer types ( and ), and .
For a detailed explanation of C-style printf format strings and their specifiers, see .
Consider using instead when possible, as it may be more efficient. In many cases, you can use C# string interpolation to construct the message before logging.

member.summary: Logs a with this log category and
member.param: The message to be logged

remarks.para: The parameter is treated as a C-style printf format string. This means that any % characters are interpreted as format specifiers. To include a literal % character in the output, you must escape it by writing %%.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.LogCategoryExtensions.<G>$403AC6335D0F64C41E9360290F705BC6.LogCritical(System.String,System.ReadOnlySpan{System.Object})
member.summary: Logs a string with this log category and
member.param: The C-style printf format string
The arguments corresponding to the format specifiers in

remarks.para: The parameter is interpreted as a C-style printf format string, and the parameter supplies the values for its format specifiers. Supported argument types include all integral types up to 64-bit (including and ), floating point types ( and ), pointer types ( and ), and .
For a detailed explanation of C-style printf format strings and their specifiers, see .
Consider using instead when possible, as it may be more efficient. In many cases, you can use C# string interpolation to construct the message before logging.

member.summary: Provides mutable data for the event

member.name: P:Sdl3Sharp.LogOutputEventArgs.Handled
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether a handled the logging message it was invoked with
member.value: A value indicating whether a handled its logging message

remarks.para: Inside a : set this propery to to indicate that the handled the given logging message.
It is possible that a is invoked with a logging message which was already handled by a previous . You can check for this inside a by examine if the value of is already set to .
If a logging message remains unhandled after a completed raise of (that is is ) and is set to , the logging messages will be passed to the default SDL logging output.

member.summary: Set the text prepended to log messages of a given priority
member.param: the SDL_LogPriority to modify
the prefix to use for that log priority, or NULL to use no prefix

remarks.para: By default SDL_LOG_PRIORITY_INFO and below have no prefix, and SDL_LOG_PRIORITY_WARN and higher have a prefix showing their priority, e.g. "WARNING: ".

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Properties.TryLock
member.summary: Tries to lock the group of properties
member.returns: if the group of properties was successfully locked; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: Obtains a multi-threaded lock for these properties. Other threads will wait while trying to lock these properties until they are unlocked. Properties must be unlocked before they are destroyed.

The lock is automatically taken when setting individual properties, calling this method is only needed when you want to set several properties atomically or want to guarantee that properties being queried aren't freed in another thread.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Properties.TrySetPointerValue(System.String,System.IntPtr,System.Action{System.IntPtr})
member.summary: Tries to set the pointer value of a property with a specified within the group of properties and potentially add the property to the group of properties, when it not yet exists. This sets the pointer value of the property together with a callback that is called when the property is deleted.
member.param: The name of the property to set
The new value of the property, or (()) to delete the property
The callback to call when the property is deleted, or if no cleanup is necessary
member.returns: if the pointer value of the property was successfully set within the group of properties; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: The callback is also called if setting the property fails for any reason.
For simply setting basic data types, like numbers, bools, or strings, use , , or instead, as those methods will handle cleanup on your behalf. This method is only for more complex, custom data.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Properties.SDL_CopyProperties(System.UInt32,System.UInt32)
member.summary: Copy a group of properties
member.param: the properties to copy
the destination properties
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks: Copy all the properties from one group of properties to another, with the exception of properties requiring cleanup (set using SDL_SetPointerPropertyWithCleanup()), which will not be copied. Any property that already exists on dst will be overwritten.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Properties.SDL_LockProperties(System.UInt32)
member.summary: Lock a group of properties
member.param: the properties to lock
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
Obtain a multi-threaded lock for these properties. Other threads will wait while trying to lock these properties until they are unlocked. Properties must be unlocked before they are destroyed.
The lock is automatically taken when setting individual properties, this function is only needed when you want to set several properties atomically or want to guarantee that properties being queried aren't freed in another thread.

member.summary: Set a pointer property in a group of properties with a cleanup function that is called when the property is deleted
member.param: the properties to modify
the name of the property to modify

the function to call when this property is deleted, or NULL if no cleanup is necessary
a pointer that is passed to the cleanup function
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
The cleanup function is also called if setting the property fails for any reason.
For simply setting basic data types, like numbers, bools, or strings, use SDL_SetNumberProperty, SDL_SetBooleanProperty, or SDL_SetStringProperty instead, as those functions will handle cleanup on your behalf. This function is only for more complex, custom data.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.Builder.TrySetMetadata(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Tries to set metadata about your app
member.param: The name of the metadata
The value of the metadata, or to remove that metadata
member.returns: if the value of the metadata was successfully set; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: You can optionally provide metadata about your app to SDL. This is not required, but strongly encouraged.

There are several locations where SDL can make use of metadata (an "About" box in the macOS menu bar, the name of the app can be shown on some audio mixers, etc). Any piece of metadata can be left out, if a specific detail doesn't make sense for the app.
See for a overview over the available metadata properties and their meanings.
Multiple calls to this method with the same value are allowed, but various state might not change once it has been already set up.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.Builder.TrySetMetadata(System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Tries to set basic metadata about your app
member.param: The name of the application ("My Game 2: Bad Guy's Revenge!")
The version of the application ("1.0.0beta5" or a git hash, or whatever makes sense)
A unique string in reverse-domain format that identifies this app ("com.example.mygame2")
member.returns: if the value of the metadata values are successfully set; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: You can optionally provide metadata about your app to SDL. This is not required, but strongly encouraged.

There are several locations where SDL can make use of metadata (an "About" box in the macOS menu bar, the name of the app can be shown on some audio mixers, etc). Any piece of metadata can be left out as a value, if a specific detail doesn't make sense for the app.
Passing a value removes any previous metadata.
Multiple calls to this method are allowed, but various state might not change once it has been already set up.
This is a simplified interface for the most important information. You can supply significantly more detailed metadata with .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.#ctor(Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.BuildAction)
member.summary: Creates a new instance and initializes SDL
member.param: A that is performed right before initializing SDL. Use the provided argument to perfom some preliminaries before SDL gets initialized.

remarks.para: Creating a instance initializes SDL (while disposing it quits SDL).
There can be only a single instance of at a time! Trying to create another one, while one already exist, results in an .

The file I/O (for example: ) and threading () subsystems are initialized by default. Message boxes ( and ) also attempt to work without initializing the video sub system, in hopes of being useful in showing an error dialog even before SDL initializes correclty. Logging (such as ) works without initialization, too.
You must specifically initialize other subsystems (either by using or by using ), if you use them in your application.
Consider reporting some basic metadata about your application inside the , by using one of the *SetMetadata methods.

In contrast to most of the remaining API which uses the Try-method pattern, this constructor intentionally fails by throwing an exception. If you want to handle failures wrap the call to this constructor in a -block, and check for more information when ing a .

member.exception: There already exists an instance of
Couldn't initialize SDL (check for more information)

member.name: P:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.Revision
member.summary: Gets the revision string of the currently loaded native SDL library
member.value: The revision string of the currently loaded native SDL library

remarks.para: The revision is arbitrary string (a hash value) uniquely identifying the exact revision of the SDL library in use, and is only useful in comparing against other revisions. It is NOT an incrementing number.
If SDL wasn't built from a git repository with the appropriate tools, this will return an empty string.
You shouldn't use this value for anything but logging it for debugging purposes. The string is not intended to be reliable in any way.

member.summary: Gets the set of environment variables for the current process
member.value: The set of environment variables for the current process, if those could get successfully retrieved; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: This set of environment variables is initialized at application start and is not affected by external calls to modify process environments (e.g. setenv() or unsetenv()) after that point.
To modify this set of environment variables use or . Changes made in this way will not persist outside of SDL, and especially not after SDL is shut down.
If you want for changes to persist in the C runtime environment after SDL is shut down, use or .

remarks.para: It's important to notice, that this method will always dispose the current instance after it finishes executing the ! This is unavoidable!

You might be able to reuse the provided after this method returns, but you can't reuse this instance of (as SDL will be shut down). If you want to use SDL after a call to this method, you must create a new instance and use that one instead.

member.name: P:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.EventFilter
member.summary: Gets or sets the global event filter
member.value: The global event filter

remarks.para: The global event filter is used for filtering all events before they get pushed onto SDL's event queue.
Returning from the filter will prevent the event from being pushed onto the event queue.
The filter is called for every event that gets pushed onto the event queue using , but events added to the queue using bypass the filter.
Disabled events will never be passed to the filter nor will they be added to the event queue.

You can use this property to chain multiple filters together by getting the current filter as a delegate, and then setting a new filter delegate that calls the previous filter as part of its implementation.

WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event filter, as it may run in a different thread! The exception to that is the handling of , which is guaranteed to be sent from the OS on the main thread and you are expected to redraw your window in response to this event.

member.name: E:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.EventWatch
member.summary: Occurs when an event is pushed onto the event queue

remarks.para: The event handler to this event is called for every event that gets pushed onto SDL's event queue using , but not for disabled events, nor for events that were filtered out by the , nor for events added to the queue using .
WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event handler to this event, as it may run in a different thread!

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.FilterEvents(Sdl3Sharp.EventFilter)
member.summary: Filters events on the current event queue using the specified filter
member.param: The event filter to apply
member.exception: is

remarks.para: Unlike the property, this method only applies the filter to events that are already in the event queue once and potentially removes some of them. It doesn't apply permanently.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.FlushEvents
member.summary: Clears all events from the current event queue

remarks.para: This will unconditionally remove any events from the event queue. If you need to remove specific events, use or instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this method.
This method only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call on the main thread immediately before calling this method.
If you have user defined events with custom data that needs to be handled (e.g. disposed or freed), you should use or to retrieve and handle those events before flushing.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.FlushEvents(Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Clears events of the specified type from the current event queue
member.param: The type of events to clear

remarks.para: This will unconditionally remove any events from the event queue that match the specified . If you need to remove a specific range of event types, use instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this method.
This method only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call on the main thread immediately before calling this method.
If you have user defined events with custom data that needs to be handled (e.g. disposed or freed), you should use or to retrieve and handle those events before flushing.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.FlushEvents(Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType,Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Clears events in the specified type range from the current event queue
member.param: The inclusive lower bound of the event type range to be cleared
The inclusive upper bound of the event type range to be cleared

remarks.para: This will unconditionally remove any events from the event queue in the range between and , inclusive. If you need to remove a specific single event type only, use instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this method.
This method only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call on the main thread immediately before calling this method.
If you have user defined events with custom data that needs to be handled (e.g. disposed or freed), you should use or to retrieve and handle those events before flushing.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.PumpEvents
member.summary: Pumps the event loop, gathering events from the input devices

remarks.para: This method updates the event queue and internal input device state.

This method gathers all the pending input information from devices and places it in the event queue. Without calls to no events would ever be placed on the queue. Often the need for calls to is hidden from the user since methods like and implicitly call . However, if you are not polling or waiting for events (e.g. you are filtering them), then you must call to force an event queue update.
You can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode (most probably that's the main thread).

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.TryPollEvent
member.summary: Tries to poll for pending events from the current event queue
member.returns: , if there is a pending event in the event queue; otherwise,

remarks.para: If there is a pending event in the current event queue, it will not be removed from the event queue, and this method will simply return . In the case this method returned , there was no pending event in the current event queue. The event queue remains unchanged in any case. Therefore you can use this method to check if there are pending events in the current event queue.
As this method may implicitly call , you can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode.

Note that Windows (and possibly other platforms) has a quirk about how it handles events while dragging/resizing a window, which can cause this function to block for significant amounts of time. Technical explanations and solutions are discussed on the wiki: .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.TryPollEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event@)
member.summary: Tries to poll for pending events from the current event queue
member.param: The next pending event from the event queue, if this method returns
member.returns: , if there was a pending event in the event queue and it was copied into ; otherwise,

remarks.para: If there is a pending event in the current event queue, it will be copied into the argument and removed from the event queue, and then this method will return . In the case this method returned , there was no pending event in the current event queue and the event queue remained unchanged.
As this method may implicitly call , you can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode.
is the favored way of receiving system events since it can be done from the main loop and does not suspend the main loop while waiting on an event to be posted.
The common practice is to fully process the current event queue once every frame, usually as a first step before updating the game's state.

Note that Windows (and possibly other platforms) has a quirk about how it handles events while dragging/resizing a window, which can cause this function to block for significant amounts of time. Technical explanations and solutions are discussed on the wiki: .

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.TryPushEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event@)
member.summary: Tries to add a new event to the current event queue
member.param: The event to add to the queue
member.returns: , if the event was successfully added to the queue; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: Events that are supposed to be added via this method are passed to the and are susceptible to being disabled. If the event is filtered out, this method will return (use to check if the event was filtered out or if there was another kind of failure). If you want to bypass the filter you can use instead.
Another common reason for this method failing is the event queue being full.

The event queue can actually be used as a two way communication channel. Not only can events be read from the queue, but the user can also push their own events onto it. The will be copied into the queue, and afterwards user can forget about it until it's (polled) by themselves or somewhere else.
Note: Pushing device input events onto the queue doesn't modify the state of the device within SDL.
For pushing user defined custom events, please use or to get event types that does not conflict with other code that also wants its own custom event types.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.TryWaitForEvent
member.summary: Tries to wait for pending events from the current event queue indefinitely
member.returns: , if there was a pending event received in the event queue; otherwise, (check for more information)

remarks.para: If there is a pending event recieved in the current event queue, it will not be removed from the event queue, and this method will simply return . In the case this method returned , there was an error while waiting and you should check for more information. The event queue remains unchanged in any case. Therefore you can use this method to block and wait until there are pending events recieved in the current event queue.
As this method may implicitly call , you can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode.

remarks.para: If there is a pending event recieved in the current event queue, it will be copied into the argument and removed from the event queue, and then this method will return . In the case this method returned , there was an error while waiting and you should check for more information.
As this method may implicitly call , you can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.TryWaitForEvent(System.Int32)
member.summary: Tries to wait until a specified timeout for pending events from the current event queue
member.param: The timeout in milliseconds
member.returns: , if there was a pending event recieved in the event queue before the timeout; otherwise,

remarks.para: If there is a pending event recieved in the current event queue before the timeout, it will not be removed from the event queue, and this method will simply return . In the case this method returned , there was no pending event recieved in the current event queue until the specified timeout. The event queue remains unchanged in any case. Therefore you can use this method to block and wait until there are pending events recieved in the current event queue.
As this method may implicitly call , you can only call this method in the thread that set the video mode.
The specified timeout is not guaranteed, the actual wait time could be longer due to system scheduling.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_AddEventWatch(,System.Void*)
member.summary: Add a callback to be triggered when an event is added to the event queue
member.param: An SDL_EventFilter function to call when an event happens
A pointer that is passed to
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

remarks.para: will be called when an event happens, and its return value is ignored.
WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event filter function, as it may run in a different thread!
If the quit event is generated by a signal (e.g. SIGINT), it will bypass the internal queue and be delivered to the watch callback immediately, and arrive at the next event poll.

Note: the callback is called for events posted by the user through SDL_PushEvent(), but not for disabled events, nor for events by a filter callback set with SDL_SetEventFilter(), nor for events posted by the user through SDL_PeepEvents().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_FilterEvents(,System.Void*)
member.summary: Run a specific filter function on the current event queue, removing any events for which the filter returns false
member.param: The SDL_EventFilter function to call when an event happens
A pointer that is passed to

remarks.para: See SDL_SetEventFilter() for more information. Unlike SDL_SetEventFilter(), this function does not change the filter permanently, it only uses the supplied filter until this function returns.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_FlushEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Clear events of a specific type from the event queue
member.param: The type of event to be cleared; see SDL_EventType for details

remarks.para: This will unconditionally remove any events from the queue that match type. If you need to remove a range of event types, use SDL_FlushEvents() instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this function.

This function only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call SDL_PumpEvents() on the main thread immediately before the flush call.
If you have user events with custom data that needs to be freed, you should use SDL_PeepEvents() to remove and clean up those events before calling this function.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_FlushEvents(Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType,Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Clear events of a range of types from the event queue
member.param: The low end of event type to be cleared, inclusive; see SDL_EventType for details
The high end of event type to be cleared, inclusive; see SDL_EventType for details

remarks.para: This will unconditionally remove any events from the queue that are in the range of to , inclusive. If you need to remove a single event type, use SDL_FlushEvent() instead.
It's also normal to just ignore events you don't care about in your event loop without calling this function.

This function only affects currently queued events. If you want to make sure that all pending OS events are flushed, you can call SDL_PumpEvents() on the main thread immediately before the flush call.

member.summary: Query the current event filter
member.param: The current callback function will be stored here
The pointer that is passed to the current event filter will be stored here
member.returns: Returns true on success or false if there is no event filter set

remarks.para: This function can be used to "chain" filters, by saving the existing filter before replacing it with a function that will call that saved filter.

member.returns: Returns true if events matching are present, or false if events matching are not present

remarks.para: If you need to check for a range of event types, use SDL_HasEvents() instead.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_HasEvents(Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType,Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Check for the existence of certain event types in the event queue

member.returns: Returns true if events with type >= and <= are present, or false if not

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_PeepEvents(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event*,System.Int32,Sdl3Sharp.EventAction,Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType,Sdl3Sharp.Events.EventType)
member.summary: Check the event queue for messages and optionally return them
member.param: Destination buffer for the retrieved events, may be NULL to leave the events in the queue and return the number of events that would have been stored
If is SDL_ADDEVENT, the number of events to add back to the event queue; if is SDL_PEEKEVENT or SDL_GETEVENT, the maximum number of events to retrieve
Action to take; see Remarks for details
Minimum value of the event type to be considered; SDL_EVENT_FIRST is a safe choice
Maximum value of the event type to be considered; SDL_EVENT_LAST is a safe choice
member.returns: Returns the number of events actually stored or -1 on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

remarks.para:
may be any of the following:
SDL_ADDEVENT Up to events will be added to the back of the event queue
SDL_PEEKEVENT
events at the front of the event queue, within the specified minimum and maximum type, will be returned to the caller and will not be removed from the queue. If you pass NULL for , then is ignored and the total number of matching events will be returned.
SDL_GETEVENT Up to events at the front of the event queue, within the specified minimum and maximum type, will be returned to the caller and will be removed from the queue
You may have to call SDL_PumpEvents() before calling this function. Otherwise, the events may not be ready to be filtered when you call SDL_PeepEvents().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_PollEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event*)
member.summary: Poll for currently pending events
member.param: The SDL_Event structure to be filled with the next event from the queue, or NULL
member.returns: Returns true if this got an event or false if there are none available

remarks.para: If is not NULL, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the SDL_Event structure pointed to by . The 1 returned refers to this event, immediately stored in the SDL Event structure -- not an event to follow.
If is NULL, it simply returns 1 if there is an event in the queue, but will not remove it from the queue.
As this function may implicitly call SDL_PumpEvents(), you can only call this function in the thread that set the video mode.
SDL_PollEvent() is the favored way of receiving system events since it can be done from the main loop and does not suspend the main loop while waiting on an event to be posted.
This function should only be called on the main thread.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_PumpEvents
member.summary: Pump the event loop, gathering events from the input devices

remarks.para: This function updates the event queue and internal input device state.

SDL_PumpEvents() gathers all the pending input information from devices and places it in the event queue. Without calls to SDL_PumpEvents() no events would ever be placed on the queue. Often the need for calls to SDL_PumpEvents() is hidden from the user since SDL_PollEvent() and SDL_WaitEvent() implicitly call SDL_PumpEvents(). However, if you are not polling or waiting for events (e.g. you are filtering them), then you must call SDL_PumpEvents() to force an event queue update.
This function should only be called on the main thread.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_PushEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event*)
member.summary: Add an event to the event queue
member.param: The SDL_Event to be added to the queue
member.returns: Returns true on success, false if the event was filtered or on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information. A common reason for error is the event queue being full.

remarks.para: The event queue can actually be used as a two way communication channel. Not only can events be read from the queue, but the user can also push their own events onto it. is a pointer to the event structure you wish to push onto the queue. The event is copied into the queue, and the caller may dispose of the memory pointed to after SDL_PushEvent() returns.
Note: Pushing device input events onto the queue doesn't modify the state of the device within SDL.
Note: Events pushed onto the queue with SDL_PushEvent() get passed through the event filter but events added with SDL_PeepEvents() do not.
For pushing application-specific events, please use SDL_RegisterEvents() to get an event type that does not conflict with other code that also wants its own custom event types.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_RemoveEventWatch(,System.Void*)
member.summary: Remove an event watch callback added with SDL_AddEventWatch()
member.param: The function originally passed to SDL_AddEventWatch()
The pointer originally passed to SDL_AddEventWatch()
member.remarks: This function takes the same input as SDL_AddEventWatch() to identify and delete the corresponding callback.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_SetEventFilter(,System.Void*)
member.summary: Set up a filter to process all events before they are added to the internal event queue
member.param: An SDL_EventFilter function to call when an event happens
A pointer that is passed to

remarks.para: If you just want to see events without modifying them or preventing them from being queued, you should use SDL_AddEventWatch() instead.

If the filter function returns true when called, then the event will be added to the internal queue. If it returns false, then the event will be dropped from the queue, but the internal state will still be updated. This allows selective filtering of dynamically arriving events.
WARNING: Be very careful of what you do in the event filter function, as it may run in a different thread!
On platforms that support it, if the quit event is generated by an interrupt signal (e.g. pressing Ctrl-C), it will be delivered to the application at the next event poll.
Note: Disabled events never make it to the event filter function; see SDL_SetEventEnabled().
Note: Events pushed onto the queue with SDL_PushEvent() get passed through the event filter, but events pushed onto the queue with SDL_PeepEvents() do not.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_WaitEvent(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event*)
member.summary: Wait indefinitely for the next available event
member.param: The SDL_Event structure to be filled in with the next event from the queue, or NULL.
member.returns: Returns true on success or false if there was an error while waiting for events; call SDL_GetError() for more information.

remarks.para: If is not NULL, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the SDL_Event structure pointed to by event.
As this function may implicitly call SDL_PumpEvents(), you can only call this function in the thread that initialized the video subsystem.
This function should only be called on the main thread.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_WaitEventTimeout(Sdl3Sharp.Events.Event*,System.Int32)
member.summary: Wait until the specified timeout (in milliseconds) for the next available event
member.param: The SDL_Event structure to be filled in with the next event from the queue, or NULL
The maximum number of milliseconds to wait for the next available event
member.returns: Returns true if this got an event or false if the timeout elapsed without any events available

remarks.para: If is not NULL, the next event is removed from the queue and stored in the SDL_Event structure pointed to by event.
As this function may implicitly call SDL_PumpEvents(), you can only call this function in the thread that initialized the video subsystem.
The timeout is not guaranteed, the actual wait time could be longer due to system scheduling.
This function should only be called on the main thread.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_EnterAppMainCallbacks(System.Int32,System.Byte**,,,,)
member.summary: An entry point for SDL's use in SDL_MAIN_USE_CALLBACKS
member.param: standard Unix main argc
standard Unix main argv
the application's SDL_AppInit function
the application's SDL_AppIterate function
the application's SDL_AppEvent function
the application's SDL_AppQuit function
member.returns: Returns standard Unix main return value
member.remarks:
Generally, you should not call this function directly. This only exists to hand off work into SDL as soon as possible, where it has a lot more control and functionality available, and make the inline code in SDL_main.h as small as possible.
Not all platforms use this, it's actual use is hidden in a magic header-only library, and you should not call this directly unless you really know what you're doing.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_GetAppMetadataProperty(System.Byte*)
member.summary: Get metadata about your app
member.param: the name of the metadata property to get
member.returns: Returns the current value of the metadata property, or the default if it is not set, NULL for properties with no default
member.remarks: This returns metadata previously set using SDL_SetAppMetadata() or SDL_SetAppMetadataProperty(). See SDL_SetAppMetadataProperty() for the list of available properties and their meanings.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_GetGlobalProperties
member.summary: Get the global SDL properties
member.returns: Returns a valid property ID on success or 0 on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_GetRevision
member.summary: Get the code revision of SDL that is linked against your program
member.returns: Returns an arbitrary string, uniquely identifying the exact revision of the SDL library in use
member.remarks:
This value is the revision of the code you are linked with and may be different from the code you are compiling with, which is found in the constant SDL_REVISION.
The revision is arbitrary string (a hash value) uniquely identifying the exact revision of the SDL library in use, and is only useful in comparing against other revisions. It is NOT an incrementing number.
If SDL wasn't built from a git repository with the appropriate tools, this will return an empty string.
You shouldn't use this function for anything but logging it for debugging purposes. The string is not intended to be reliable in any way.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_GetVersion
member.summary: Get the version of SDL that is linked against your program
member.returns: Returns the version of the linked library
member.remarks:
If you are linking to SDL dynamically, then it is possible that the current version will be different than the version you compiled against. This function returns the current version, while SDL_VERSION is the version you compiled with.
This function may be called safely at any time, even before SDL_Init().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_Init(Sdl3Sharp.SubSystems)
member.summary: Initialize the SDL library
member.param: subsystem initialization flags
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
SDL_Init() simply forwards to calling SDL_InitSubSystem(). Therefore, the two may be used interchangeably. Though for readability of your code SDL_InitSubSystem() might be preferred. The file I/O (for example: SDL_IOFromFile) and threading (SDL_CreateThread) subsystems are initialized by default. Message boxes (SDL_ShowSimpleMessageBox) also attempt to work without initializing the video subsystem, in hopes of being useful in showing an error dialog when SDL_Init fails. You must specifically initialize other subsystems if you use them in your application.
Logging (such as SDL_Log) works without initialization, too.
flags may be any of the following OR'd together:
SDL_INIT_AUDIO audio subsystem; automatically initializes the events subsystem
SDL_INIT_VIDEO video subsystem; automatically initializes the events subsystem, should be initialized on the main thread.
SDL_INIT_JOYSTICK joystick subsystem; automatically initializes the events subsystem
SDL_INIT_HAPTIC haptic (force feedback) subsystem
SDL_INIT_GAMEPAD gamepad subsystem; automatically initializes the joystick subsystem
SDL_INIT_EVENTS events subsystem
SDL_INIT_SENSOR sensor subsystem; automatically initializes the events subsystem
SDL_INIT_CAMERA camera subsystem; automatically initializes the events subsystem
Subsystem initialization is ref-counted, you must call SDL_QuitSubSystem() for each SDL_InitSubSystem() to correctly shutdown a subsystem manually (or call SDL_Quit() to force shutdown). If a subsystem is already loaded then this call will increase the ref-count and return.
Consider reporting some basic metadata about your application before calling SDL_Init, using either SDL_SetAppMetadata() or SDL_SetAppMetadataProperty().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_Quit
member.summary: Clean up all initialized subsystems
member.remarks:
You should call this function even if you have already shutdown each initialized subsystem with SDL_QuitSubSystem(). It is safe to call this function even in the case of errors in initialization.
You can use this function with atexit() to ensure that it is run when your application is shutdown, but it is not wise to do this from a library or other dynamically loaded code.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_RunApp(System.Int32,System.Byte**,,System.Void*)
member.summary: Initializes and launches an SDL application, by doing platform-specific initialization before calling your mainFunction and cleanups after it returns, if that is needed for a specific platform, otherwise it just calls mainFunction
member.param: the argc parameter from the application's main() function, or 0 if the platform's main-equivalent has no argc
the argv parameter from the application's main() function, or NULL if the platform's main-equivalent has no argv
your SDL app's C-style main(). NOT the function you're calling this from! Its name doesn't matter; it doesn't literally have to be main.
should be NULL (reserved for future use, will probably be platform-specific then)
member.returns: Returns the return value from mainFunction: 0 on success, otherwise failure; SDL_GetError() might have more information on the failure
member.remarks: You can use this if you want to use your own main() implementation without using SDL_main (like when using SDL_MAIN_HANDLED). When using this, you do not need SDL_SetMainReady().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_SetAppMetadata(System.Byte*,System.Byte*,System.Byte*)
member.summary: Specify basic metadata about your app
member.param: The name of the application ("My Game 2: Bad Guy's Revenge!")
The version of the application ("1.0.0beta5" or a git hash, or whatever makes sense)
A unique string in reverse-domain format that identifies this app ("com.example.mygame2")
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
You can optionally provide metadata about your app to SDL. This is not required, but strongly encouraged.
There are several locations where SDL can make use of metadata (an "About" box in the macOS menu bar, the name of the app can be shown on some audio mixers, etc). Any piece of metadata can be left as NULL, if a specific detail doesn't make sense for the app.
This function should be called as early as possible, before SDL_Init. Multiple calls to this function are allowed, but various state might not change once it has been set up with a previous call to this function.
Passing a NULL removes any previous metadata.
This is a simplified interface for the most important information. You can supply significantly more detailed metadata with SDL_SetAppMetadataProperty().

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_SetAppMetadataProperty(System.Byte*,System.Byte*)
member.summary: Specify metadata about your app through a set of properties
member.param: the name of the metadata property to set
the value of the property, or NULL to remove that property
member.returns: Returns true on success or false on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
You can optionally provide metadata about your app to SDL. This is not required, but strongly encouraged.
There are several locations where SDL can make use of metadata (an "About" box in the macOS menu bar, the name of the app can be shown on some audio mixers, etc). Any piece of metadata can be left out, if a specific detail doesn't make sense for the app.
This function should be called as early as possible, before SDL_Init. Multiple calls to this function are allowed, but various state might not change once it has been set up with a previous call to this function.
Once set, this metadata can be read using SDL_GetAppMetadataProperty().
These are the supported properties:
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_NAME_STRING The human-readable name of the application, like "My Game 2: Bad Guy's Revenge!". This will show up anywhere the OS shows the name of the application separately from window titles, such as volume control applets, etc. This defaults to "SDL Application".
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_VERSION_STRING The version of the app that is running; there are no rules on format, so "1.0.3beta2" and "April 22nd, 2024" and a git hash are all valid options. This has no default.
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_IDENTIFIER_STRING A unique string that identifies this app. This must be in reverse-domain format, like "com.example.mygame2". This string is used by desktop compositors to identify and group windows together, as well as match applications with associated desktop settings and icons. If you plan to package your application in a container such as Flatpak, the app ID should match the name of your Flatpak container as well. This has no default.
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_CREATOR_STRING The human-readable name of the creator/developer/maker of this app, like "MojoWorkshop, LLC"
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_COPYRIGHT_STRING The human-readable copyright notice, like "Copyright (c) 2024 MojoWorkshop, LLC" or whatnot. Keep this to one line, don't paste a copy of a whole software license in here. This has no default.
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_URL_STRING A URL to the app on the web. Maybe a product page, or a storefront, or even a GitHub repository, for user's further information This has no default.
SDL_PROP_APP_METADATA_TYPE_STRING The type of application this is. Currently this string can be "game" for a video game, "mediaplayer" for a media player, or generically "application" if nothing else applies. Future versions of SDL might add new types. This defaults to "application".

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Sdl.SDL_WasInit(Sdl3Sharp.SubSystems)
member.summary: Get a mask of the specified subsystems which are currently initialized
member.param: any of the flags used by SDL_Init(); see SDL_Init for details
member.returns: Returns a mask of all initialized subsystems if is 0, otherwise it returns the initialization status of the specified subsystems

member.returns: Returns a new condition variable or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information

member.summary: Destroy a condition variable

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Condition.SDL_WaitCondition(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Condition.SDL_Condition*,Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*)
member.summary: Wait until a condition variable is signaled
member.param: the condition variable to wait on
the mutex used to coordinate thread access
member.remarks:
This function unlocks the specified mutex and waits for another thread to call SDL_SignalCondition() or SDL_BroadcastCondition() on the condition variable cond. Once the condition variable is signaled, the mutex is re-locked and the function returns.
The mutex must be locked before calling this function. Locking the mutex recursively (more than once) is not supported and leads to undefined behavior.
This function is the equivalent of calling SDL_WaitConditionTimeout() with a time length of -1.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Condition.SDL_WaitConditionTimeout(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Condition.SDL_Condition*,Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*,System.Int32)
member.summary: Wait until a condition variable is signaled or a certain time has passed
member.param: the condition variable to wait on
the mutex used to coordinate thread access
the maximum time to wait, in milliseconds, or -1 to wait indefinitely
member.returns: Returns true if the condition variable is signaled, false if the condition is not signaled in the allotted time
member.remarks:
This function unlocks the specified mutex and waits for another thread to call SDL_SignalCondition() or SDL_BroadcastCondition() on the condition variable cond, or for the specified time to elapse. Once the condition variable is signaled or the time elapsed, the mutex is re-locked and the function returns.
The mutex must be locked before calling this function. Locking the mutex recursively (more than once) is not supported and leads to undefined behavior.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.MemoryBarrier.SDL_MemoryBarrierAcquireFunction
member.summary: Insert a memory acquire barrier (function version)
member.remarks:
Memory barriers are designed to prevent reads and writes from being reordered by the compiler and being seen out of order on multi-core CPUs.
A typical pattern would be for thread A to write some data and a flag, and for thread B to read the flag and get the data. In this case you would insert a release barrier between writing the data and the flag, guaranteeing that the data write completes no later than the flag is written, and you would insert an acquire barrier between reading the flag and reading the data, to ensure that all the reads associated with the flag have completed.
In this pattern you should always see a release barrier paired with an acquire barrier and you should gate the data reads/writes with a single flag variable.
For more information on these semantics, take a look at the blog post: http://preshing.com/20120913/acquire-and-release-semantics

members.member:
Insert a memory release barrier (function version).
/// Memory barriers are designed to prevent reads and writes from being reordered by the compiler and being seen out of order on multi-core CPUs.
A typical pattern would be for thread A to write some data and a flag, and for thread B to read the flag and get the data. In this case you would insert a release barrier between writing the data and the flag, guaranteeing that the data write completes no later than the flag is written, and you would insert an acquire barrier between reading the flag and reading the data, to ensure that all the reads associated with the flag have completed.
In this pattern you should always see a release barrier paired with an acquire barrier and you should gate the data reads/writes with a single flag variable.
For more information on these semantics, take a look at the blog post: http://preshing.com/20120913/acquire-and-release-semantics
SDL_MemoryBarrierReleaseFunction

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_CreateMutex
member.summary: Create a new mutex
member.returns: Returns the initialized and unlocked mutex or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
All newly-created mutexes begin in the unlocked state.
Calls to SDL_LockMutex() will not return while the mutex is locked by another thread. See SDL_TryLockMutex() to attempt to lock without blocking.
SDL mutexes are reentrant.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_DestroyMutex(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*)
member.summary: Destroy a mutex created with SDL_CreateMutex()
member.param: the mutex to destroy
member.remarks: This function must be called on any mutex that is no longer needed. Failure to destroy a mutex will result in a system memory or resource leak. While it is safe to destroy a mutex that is unlocked, it is not safe to attempt to destroy a locked mutex, and may result in undefined behavior depending on the platform.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_LockMutex(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*)
member.summary: Lock the mutex
member.param: the mutex to lock
member.remarks:
This will block until the mutex is available, which is to say it is in the unlocked state and the OS has chosen the caller as the next thread to lock it. Of all threads waiting to lock the mutex, only one may do so at a time.
It is legal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked mutex. It must unlock it the same number of times before it is actually made available for other threads in the system (this is known as a "recursive mutex").
This function does not fail; if mutex is NULL, it will return immediately having locked nothing. If the mutex is valid, this function will always block until it can lock the mutex, and return with it locked.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_TryLockMutex(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*)
member.summary: Try to lock a mutex without blocking
member.param: the mutex to try to lock
member.returns: Returns true on success, false if the mutex would block
member.remarks:
This works just like SDL_LockMutex(), but if the mutex is not available, this function returns false immediately.
This technique is useful if you need exclusive access to a resource but don't want to wait for it, and will return to it to try again later.
This function returns true if passed a NULL mutex.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_UnlockMutex(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Mutex.SDL_Mutex*)
member.summary: Unlock the mutex
member.param: the mutex to unlock
member.remarks:
It is legal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked mutex. It must unlock it the same number of times before it is actually made available for other threads in the system (this is known as a "recursive mutex").
It is illegal to unlock a mutex that has not been locked by the current thread, and doing so results in undefined behavior.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_CreateRWLock
member.summary: Create a new read/write lock
member.returns: Returns the initialized and unlocked read/write lock or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks:
A read/write lock is useful for situations where you have multiple threads trying to access a resource that is rarely updated. All threads requesting a read-only lock will be allowed to run in parallel; if a thread requests a write lock, it will be provided exclusive access. This makes it safe for multiple threads to use a resource at the same time if they promise not to change it, and when it has to be changed, the rwlock will serve as a gateway to make sure those changes can be made safely.
In the right situation, a rwlock can be more efficient than a mutex, which only lets a single thread proceed at a time, even if it won't be modifying the data.
All newly-created read/write locks begin in the unlocked state.
Calls to SDL_LockRWLockForReading() and SDL_LockRWLockForWriting() will not return while the rwlock is locked for writing by another thread. See SDL_TryLockRWLockForReading() and SDL_TryLockRWLockForWriting() to attempt to lock without blocking.
SDL read/write locks are only recursive for read-only locks! They are not guaranteed to be fair, or provide access in a FIFO manner! They are not guaranteed to favor writers. You may not lock a rwlock for both read-only and write access at the same time from the same thread (so you can't promote your read-only lock to a write lock without unlocking first).

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_DestroyRWLock(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Destroy a read/write lock created with SDL_CreateRWLock()
member.param: the rwlock to destroy
member.remarks: This function must be called on any read/write lock that is no longer needed. Failure to destroy a rwlock will result in a system memory or resource leak. While it is safe to destroy a rwlock that is unlocked, it is not safe to attempt to destroy a locked rwlock, and may result in undefined behavior depending on the platform.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_LockRWLockForReading(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Lock the read/write lock for read only operations
member.param: the read/write lock to lock
member.remarks:
This will block until the rwlock is available, which is to say it is not locked for writing by any other thread. Of all threads waiting to lock the rwlock, all may do so at the same time as long as they are requesting read-only access; if a thread wants to lock for writing, only one may do so at a time, and no other threads, read-only or not, may hold the lock at the same time.
It is legal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked rwlock for reading. It must unlock it the same number of times before it is actually made available for other threads in the system (this is known as a "recursive rwlock").
Note that locking for writing is not recursive (this is only available to read-only locks).
It is illegal to request a read-only lock from a thread that already holds the write lock. Doing so results in undefined behavior. Unlock the write lock before requesting a read-only lock. (But, of course, if you have the write lock, you don't need further locks to read in any case.)
This function does not fail; if rwlock is NULL, it will return immediately having locked nothing. If the rwlock is valid, this function will always block until it can lock the mutex, and return with it locked.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_LockRWLockForWriting(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Lock the read/write lock for write operations
member.param: the read/write lock to lock
member.remarks:
This will block until the rwlock is available, which is to say it is not locked for reading or writing by any other thread. Only one thread may hold the lock when it requests write access; all other threads, whether they also want to write or only want read-only access, must wait until the writer thread has released the lock.
It is illegal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked rwlock for writing (read-only may be locked recursively, writing can not). Doing so results in undefined behavior.
It is illegal to request a write lock from a thread that already holds a read-only lock. Doing so results in undefined behavior. Unlock the read-only lock before requesting a write lock.
This function does not fail; if rwlock is NULL, it will return immediately having locked nothing. If the rwlock is valid, this function will always block until it can lock the mutex, and return with it locked.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_TryLockRWLockForReading(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Try to lock a read/write lock for reading without blocking
member.param: the rwlock to try to lock
member.returns: Returns true on success, false if the lock would block
member.remarks:
This works just like SDL_LockRWLockForReading(), but if the rwlock is not available, then this function returns false immediately.
This technique is useful if you need access to a resource but don't want to wait for it, and will return to it to try again later.
Trying to lock for read-only access can succeed if other threads are holding read-only locks, as this won't prevent access.
This function returns true if passed a NULL rwlock.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_TryLockRWLockForWriting(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Try to lock a read/write lock for writing without blocking
member.param: the rwlock to try to lock
member.returns: Returns true on success, false if the lock would block
member.remarks:
This works just like SDL_LockRWLockForWriting(), but if the rwlock is not available, then this function returns false immediately.
This technique is useful if you need exclusive access to a resource but don't want to wait for it, and will return to it to try again later.
It is illegal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked rwlock for writing (read-only may be locked recursively, writing can not). Doing so results in undefined behavior.
It is illegal to request a write lock from a thread that already holds a read-only lock. Doing so results in undefined behavior. Unlock the read-only lock before requesting a write lock.
This function returns true if passed a NULL rwlock.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_UnlockRWLock(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.ReadWriteLock.SDL_RWLock*)
member.summary: Unlock the read/write lock
member.param: the rwlock to unlock
member.remarks:
Use this function to unlock the rwlock, whether it was locked for read-only or write operations.
It is legal for the owning thread to lock an already-locked read-only lock. It must unlock it the same number of times before it is actually made available for other threads in the system (this is known as a "recursive rwlock").
It is illegal to unlock a rwlock that has not been locked by the current thread, and doing so results in undefined behavior.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_CreateSemaphore(System.UInt32)
member.summary: Create a semaphore
member.param: the starting value of the semaphore
member.returns: Returns a new semaphore or NULL on failure; call SDL_GetError() for more information
member.remarks: This function creates a new semaphore and initializes it with the value initial_value. Each wait operation on the semaphore will atomically decrement the semaphore value and potentially block if the semaphore value is 0. Each post operation will atomically increment the semaphore value and wake waiting threads and allow them to retry the wait operation.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_DestroySemaphore(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*)
member.summary: Destroy a semaphore
member.param: the semaphore to destroy
member.remarks: It is not safe to destroy a semaphore if there are threads currently waiting on it

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_GetSemaphoreValue(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*)
member.summary: Get the current value of a semaphore
member.param: the semaphore to query
member.returns: Returns the current value of the semaphore

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_SignalSemaphore(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*)
member.summary: Atomically increment a semaphore's value and wake waiting threads
member.param: the semaphore to increment

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_TryWaitSemaphore(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*)
member.summary: See if a semaphore has a positive value and decrement it if it does
member.param: the semaphore to wait on
member.returns: Returns true if the wait succeeds, false if the wait would block
member.remarks: This function checks to see if the semaphore pointed to by sem has a positive value and atomically decrements the semaphore value if it does. If the semaphore doesn't have a positive value, the function immediately returns false.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_WaitSemaphore(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*)
member.summary: Wait until a semaphore has a positive value and then decrements it
member.param: the semaphore wait on
member.remarks:
This function suspends the calling thread until the semaphore pointed to by sem has a positive value, and then atomically decrement the semaphore value.
This function is the equivalent of calling SDL_WaitSemaphoreTimeout() with a time length of -1.

member.name: M:Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_WaitSemaphoreTimeout(Sdl3Sharp.Threading.Semaphore.SDL_Semaphore*,System.Int32)
member.summary: Wait until a semaphore has a positive value and then decrements it
member.param: the semaphore to wait on
the length of the timeout, in milliseconds, or -1 to wait indefinitely
member.returns: Returns true if the wait succeeds or false if the wait times out
member.remarks: This function suspends the calling thread until either the semaphore pointed to by sem has a positive value or the specified time has elapsed. If the call is successful it will atomically decrement the semaphore value.
